html:
RECOUP Integrative, Root-Cause Geriatrics Program

Introduction

  • India has the second-largest geriatric population, aged 60 years or older, globally, with projections indicating that this number will double to over 20% of the total population by 2050.
  • In 2023, the elderly population in India was estimated at 153 million.
  • The estimated decadal growth rate of the elderly population in India is currently 41%, according to the India Ageing Report.
  • By 2050, the number of elderly individuals is expected to surpass that of children under 15 years old.

Challenges in the Geriatric Population

  • Chronic Diseases and Co-morbidities:
    • Cardiovascular Disease: Heart disease and stroke are major causes of mortality and morbidity in the elderly.
    • Cognitive Impairment: Dementia and other forms of cognitive decline are common, affecting memory, thinking, and overall function.
    • Falls: Falls are a leading cause of injury and disability in older adults, leading to fractures, head injuries, and fear of falling.
    • Frailty: A condition characterized by reduced physical reserves and increased vulnerability to stressors, often associated with multiple chronic conditions.
    • Mood Disorders: Depression, anxiety, and other mental health issues are prevalent in the elderly, impacting their quality of life and well-being.
    • Other Chronic Conditions: Diabetes, arthritis, vision and hearing loss, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) are also common in older adults.
  • Disabilities and Functional Limitations:
    • Mobility Problems: Challenges in walking, standing, and other movement-related tasks can greatly affect daily life.
    • Sensory Impairments: Hearing and vision loss can hinder communication, social interaction, and safety.
    • Urinary Incontinence: Loss of bladder control can lead to social isolation, embarrassment, and skin problems.
    • Multiple Disabilities: Many older adults experience multiple disabilities simultaneously, making their care needs complex.
  • Access to Healthcare and Resources:
    • Limited Access: Underserved populations often face barriers to specialized geriatric care, transportation, and healthcare facilities.
    • Inadequate insurance coverage and high claims denial rates.
    • Affordability: The costs of medications, care services, and other healthcare needs can significantly burden many older adults and their families.
    • Caregiver Strain: Families caring for elderly relatives often experience physical, emotional, and financial strain, impacting their own well-being.
    • Social Isolation and Loneliness: Living alone, having few social connections, and losing loved ones can lead to feelings of isolation and loneliness, which are associated with increased health risks.
    • Lack of Comprehensive Care: Fragmented healthcare systems, inadequate geriatric training among primary care physicians, and poor care coordination can lead to missed opportunities for preventing and managing chronic conditions. Overdependence on ectomies, implants and transplants.
    • No access to training on self-care at home; technology use for health, wellness and resiliency building.
  • Public Health Implications:
    • Preventable Morbidity and Injury: Many health events among the elderly can be prevented through health interventions such as fall prevention programs, medication management (avoiding polypharmacy), and early detection of chronic conditions.
    • Ageing Population Demands: The ageing global population places increasing demands on healthcare systems, necessitating adjustments in the workforce, facilities, and service delivery.
    • Need for Specialized Care: Geriatric care necessitates a tailored approach that addresses the unique needs of the elderly, including their complex health conditions, functional limitations, and social requirements.

At RECOUP, we recognize the unique and evolving needs of older adults. Our Geriatric Care Program offers a comprehensive, personalized approach to promoting healthy ageing and enhancing quality of life. Through a structured process and a dedicated interdisciplinary team, we empower our senior clients to achieve and maintain optimal health. We help families understand the best approaches to caring for their loved ones and enable older patients to live as independently as possible.

Our Vision

We aim to redefine how seniors experience ageing—shifting the narrative from managing decline to achieving vitality. The RECOUP Geriatric Care Program is more than a healthcare service; it is a commitment to empowering seniors.

What Sets RECOUP Apart?

Our approach goes beyond a care plan; it represents a journey toward sustained well-being. We emphasize collaborative care, ongoing support, and personalized attention to help seniors thrive physically, mentally, and emotionally. Care is delivered in a hybrid manner, partly at home and in our clinics.

The integrative, root-cause geriatric program focuses on a personalized, holistic approach to address the unique needs of older adults, going beyond traditional disease-oriented care. It involves a comprehensive assessment of the individual's health history, lifestyle, and other factors to identify root causes of physiological imbalances and develop a tailored care plan. This plan may include dietary changes, supplementation, peptide therapy, regenerative therapies, stress management, and lifestyle modifications to improve overall health and well-being.

Step-by-Step Geriatric Care Journey

Step 1: Comprehensive Health Analysis

A RECOUP client begins their health journey with an initial meeting that can be held either at their residence or in our clinic, depending on convenience and necessity. During this visit, a thorough assessment is conducted that includes:

  • Detailed clinical history
  • Biometrics and vital signs
  • Comprehensive health analysis: Structured health questionnaire covering menopause and andropause-related concerns, longevity needs, survival needs, health maintenance needs.
  • A focused geriatric physical examination, considering age-related changes and specific conditions like nutrition, vision, hearing, balance, and risk of falls.
  • Determining functional age, bioage, and organ age.
  • Musculoskeletal and neurological assessment.
  • Functional Assessments: Evaluation of activities of daily living (ADLs), instrumental activities of daily living (IADLs), cognitive function, including Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA), Katz Index and Psychological assessments.
  • Nutrition physical examination.
  • Lab Testing: This may include comprehensive blood work, micronutrient testing, gut microbiome analysis, organic acid test, and other specialized tests to identify imbalances and deficiencies. It may also include screening for cancer, Autoimmunity, Degenerative diseases, and accelerated ageing.
  • Genetic tests, including pharmacogenomics and metabolomics.
  • Home environmental assessments to identify and address potential hazards that could lead to falls, injuries, or accidents or risk factors for chronic diseases, e.g., exposure to toxins, mold, etc.

Biometrics

  1. Height
  2. Weight
  3. BMI (Body Mass Index)
  4. Waist circumference
  5. Basal metabolic rate (BMR) – if applicable
  6. VO2max
  7. Medical infrared thermography
  8. Body composition analysis (in our clinics)
  9. Cardiometabolic analyzer (in our clinics)
  10. Insulin resistance (in our clinics)
  11. Foot pressure analysis (in our clinics)
  12. Instrumented gait analysis (in our clinics)

Vital Signs

  1. Blood Pressure (BP) – sitting, standing (to assess for orthostatic hypotension)
  2. Heart Rate (Pulse)
  3. Respiratory Rate
  4. Body Temperature
  5. Oxygen Saturation (SpO2)
  6. Blood Glucose (Random / Fasting / Post-prandial)
  7. Pulse Pressure – calculated, useful in assessing arterial stiffness

Relevant Tests for the Geriatric Population

  1. ECG – if cardiac concerns or medication risks are present
  2. Grip Strength – a marker of frailty and muscle status
  3. Gait Speed / Timed Up & Go Test – to assess fall risk

RECOUP Entire Person Health Score and Care Plan Generation using AI: EPHS-S, EPHS-P, EPHS-O.

Optional referrals for oral, vision, and hearing assessments.

Step 2: Personalized Care Plan

Based on the assessment, our interdisciplinary team creates a customized care plan tailored to the individual's health status and personal goals. The plan addresses key areas such as:

  1. Vital signs and ongoing health monitoring.
  2. Cognitive Stimulation and Rehabilitation
    • Cognitive Rehabilitation Therapy (CRT): Personalized interventions to strengthen memory, attention, problem-solving, and executive functioning, especially for those at risk of cognitive decline or mild cognitive impairment (MCI).
    • Use of gamified cognitive apps and augmented reality-based training to enhance neuroplasticity and promote engagement.
  3. Grief, Loss and Identity Support
    • Therapy addressing role transitions (retirement, loss of spouse, reduced autonomy) to preserve self-worth and identity in ageing.
    • Grief counselling for bereavement and anticipatory loss is often overlooked in clinical settings.
  4. Psychotherapeutic Interventions for Mood and Personality-Related Vulnerabilities
    • Reminiscence Therapy and Narrative Therapy to help seniors reflect, integrate life experiences, and enhance emotional resilience.
    • Specialized interventions for late-onset depression, anxiety, and personality-related vulnerabilities (e.g., obsessive traits exacerbated by age-related changes).
  5. Dietary modifications and personalized meal plans to address nutritional deficiencies, improve gut health, and optimize overall well-being.
  6. Supplementation: It may be recommended under the guidance of a physician to address identified imbalances or deficiencies and slow ageing.
  7. Bioidentical hormone therapy (BHRT) is used to address hormonal imbalances associated with menopause, andropause and ageing.
  8. Addressing Physiological Imbalances in hormones, gut function, or other physiological systems to optimize overall health.
  9. Management of chronic diseases, including osteoporosis, arthritis, cardiometabolic conditions, etc.
  10. Lifestyle Modifications: Encouraging regular physical activity, stress reduction techniques, sleep hygiene, and other lifestyle adjustments to promote overall well-being.
  11. Mind-Body Practices: Incorporating practices like meditation, yoga, or tai chi to address mental and emotional well-being.
  12. Interventions to prevent frailty include regular exercise, especially resistance and balance training, Neurocognitive Imagery (Franklin method), Exergaming and gamified exercises, group activities, maintaining a healthy diet, and addressing psychosocial factors.
  13. To prevent falls, interventions should address individual risk factors through balance exercises, Tai Chi, environmental modifications, medication reviews, and multifactorial strategies.
  14. Musculoskeletal function, pain management and mobility through home visits by the physiotherapists.
  15. Specialized interdisciplinary rehabilitation programs for neurodegenerative disorders, e.g., Alzheimer's and other dementias, Parkinson's Disease, Stroke, Chronic Pain, Arthritis, etc.
  16. Rehabilitation of speech and hearing problems, swallowing difficulties, slurring, aphasia, apraxia, etc.
  17. Management of visual impairment.
  18. Regenerative therapies (e.g., ozone, molecular hydrogen, prolotherapy, platelet-rich plasma, stem cell therapy, methylene blue).
  19. Peptide therapy to address age-related declines by modulating various bodily functions. It can enhance muscle mass and strength, bone and skin health, reduce inflammation, support brain function, and potentially slow ageing.
  20. Pre- and post-hospitalization care plan.
    • Enhanced recovery from surgery (ERAS).
    • Prehabilitation and post-operative rehabilitation.
  21. Home ergonomics.
  22. Environmental care at home to reduce persistent harm from mold, bugs, toxins, pollutants, chemicals, radiation, metals and plastics.
  23. Home nursing care includes wound management, medication administration, urinary incontinence management, and vital sign monitoring.
  24. Existential Well-being and End-of-Life Psychological Support
    • Meaning-Centred Therapy and Dignity Therapy help seniors find purpose and peace in later life.
    • Psychological support for advance care planning and end-of-life discussions.

Step 3: Guided Implementation with RECOUP Health Coach

Our involvement doesn't stop with the creation of the plan. Each member is paired with a dedicated RECOUP Health Coach who:

  • Explains each component of the care plan and empowers the member with clear, actionable knowledge.
  • Offers consistent encouragement and reminders to implement recommended strategies.
  • Facilitates connections with relevant healthcare team members.
  • Coordinates any required third-party services through our concierge desk.

This process ensures that care is planned and effectively executed, making a tangible difference in the everyday lives of our members.

Step 4: Ongoing Support and Enrichment Services

To complement the core services, our members also enjoy:

  • Unlimited 24/7 doctor teleconsultations for instant medical guidance.
  • Group sessions: Smart ageing, Gamified health experiences, Storytelling, training sessions, coherence and resilience building programs. The making of the silver economy. Super ageing. Centenarians. Regular educational webinars or in-person talks covering health, wellness, and lifestyle topics tailored for seniors.
  • Regular Follow-up: Periodic check-ins to monitor progress, evaluate the effectiveness of the treatment plan, and adjust as needed.
  • Patient Empowerment: Focus on empowering patients to actively participate in their health and well-being through education, support, and self-management strategies.
  • Psychoeducation for Families on Mental Health in Ageing
    • Education on recognizing early signs of cognitive or emotional decline.
    • Guidance on how to support emotional well-being at home through communication strategies, routines, and validation.
  • Structured Support for Caregivers
    • Caregiver burnout screening and psychological first aid.
    • Caregiver support groups led by clinical psychologists provide emotional processing, guidance, and peer connection.

RECOUP's Geriatrics Team

  • Physician trained in Geriatrics
  • Orthopedic Surgeon
  • Rehabilitation and Pain Physicians
  • Regenerative Medicine Physicians
  • Orthopedic Rheumatologists
  • Functional and Lifestyle Medicine Physicians
  • Ayurveda Physician
  • Naturopathy and Yoga Physicians
  • Physiotherapists
  • Occupational Therapists
  • Speech Therapist
  • Ergonomists
  • Aquatic Therapists
  • Tai Chi and Qi Gong Therapists
  • Alexander and Feldenkrais Teachers
  • Clinical Psychologists
  • Nutritionists
  • Health Coaches
  • Nurses
  • Patient Care Coordinators
  • Other professionals, as required

Join the RECOUP Geriatric Care Program and embrace healthy ageing with expert support, compassionate care, and personalized attention every step of the way.

Sample patient report:

RECOUP Geriatric Care Plan

Patient Profile:

Name: Mr. A.B. Sharma

Age: 65 years

Sex: Male

Primary Concerns:

  • Chronic mechanical low back pain
  • Osteoarthritis of the knee (bilateral, right more affected)
  • Reduced mobility and increased fall risk
  • Needs assistance with daily functional mobility and home care support

Step 1: Comprehensive Health Analysis

Initial Visit Conducted At: Home

Key Assessments Performed:

  • Clinical History:
    • 10-year history of lower back stiffness, worse in the morning and with prolonged standing
    • Pain aggravated by bending, relieved by rest
    • Known case of osteoarthritis in both knees (X-ray evidence Grade 2–3)
    • Reports decreased physical activity over the past 2 years
    • No major cardiac or respiratory comorbidities
  • Biometrics and Vitals
    • Height: 172 cm
    • Weight: 79 kg
    • BMI: 26.7 (Overweight)
    • Waist Circumference: 96 cm
    • BP: 138/82 mmHg (Sitting), 120/78 mmHg (Standing – No orthostatic drop)
    • Pulse: 76 bpm, regular
    • SpO2: 97%
    • Random Blood Glucose: 112 mg/dL
    • Gait Speed: Slightly reduced (Timed Up & Go: 14 seconds)
    • Grip Strength: Mild reduction
  • Functional Assessments
    • Musculoskeletal: Limited lumbar flexion-extension, crepitus in knees
    • Neurological: Normal sensation and reflexes, no radiculopathy
    • Nutrition Physical Exam: Sarcopenic changes, low lean mass, mild central adiposity
    • Cognitive/Psychological: Mild depressive symptoms (PHQ-9 Score: 6), mild sleep disturbance
  • Investigations Ordered:
    • CBC, ESR, CRP, Vitamin D, Calcium, Lipid Profile, HbA1c, Renal and Liver Panel
    • X-ray Lumbar Spine (AP/Lat) & Bilateral Knee Joints
    • ECG (normal)

Step 2: Personalized Care Plan

Goals:

  • Pain reduction
  • Improved mobility and functional independence
  • Prevent progression of OA and prevent falls
  • Enhance psychological well-being and motivation
  • Sustainable activity and nutrition pattern for joint health

Care Plan Components:

  1. Pain Management
    • Topical anti-inflammatory gel + hot packs
    • Oral analgesics as needed (under physician guidance)
  2. Physiotherapy Home Visits (Daily)
    • Core strengthening exercises
    • Lower limb mobility drills
    • Balance and fall-prevention training
    • Joint protection education
  3. Nutrition Plan
    • Anti-inflammatory meal plan with omega-3s, antioxidants, and protein-rich foods
    • Calorie moderation for mild weight loss
    • Supplements: Vitamin D3, Calcium, Curcumin (as indicated)
  4. Psychological Support
    • Counselling for mood and motivation
    • Sleep hygiene techniques
  5. Wellness & Lifestyle
    • Gentle yoga for seniors (supervised, thrice weekly)
    • Guided meditation and breathing sessions
    • Encouragement to maintain social engagement
  6. Optional Services
    • Dental, vision, and hearing screening to be scheduled
    • Pharmacogenomic assessment (if pain medication resistance is observed)

Step 3: RECOUP Health Coach Support

Assigned Health Coach: Ms. Neha Rao

  • Orients the member and caregiver to the care plan
  • Sets up a daily and weekly routine tracker
  • Monitors pain, exercise adherence, mood, and meal patterns
  • Coordinates all physiotherapy sessions and lab result reviews
  • Liaises with the concierge desk for equipment rentals (walker, hot-cold therapy tools)
  • Reminders for medications, appointments, and hydration

Step 4: Ongoing Support and Enrichment Services

  • 24/7 access to the RECOUP doctor helpline
  • Weekly physiotherapist reassessments and monthly medical review
  • Monthly wellness webinars: "Managing Arthritis Naturally", "Home Safety for Seniors"
  • Quarterly reassessment of functional ability and goal achievement

Summary Outcome Goals (Next 3 months):

  • ≥30% reduction in reported pain score (VAS)
  • ≥20% improvement in Timed Up & Go test
  • Improved sleep duration and quality
  • Initiation and adherence to home exercise and dietary plan
  • Active participation in wellness and social engagement sessions
